18: pneuma kyriou ep eme hou heneken echrisen me — the Spirit of the Lord is upon me because he has anointed me — the Nazareth synagogue reading is the programmatic Christological text for Luke's Gospel. Echrisen (anointed) shares the root with Christos; Jesus identifies himself as the Spirit-anointed one of Isa 61. The four infinitives that follow specify the anointing's purpose: euangelisasthai ptochois (proclaim good news to the poor), keruxai aichmalotos aphesin (announce release to captives), aposteilai tethrausmenous en aphesei (send away the broken in release), keruxai eniauton kyriou dekton (announce the year of the Lord's favor = the Jubilee).
21: semeron peplerothe he graphe haute en tois osin hymon — today this scripture has been fulfilled in your hearing — the adverb semeron (today) is Luke's characteristic eschatological-present marker (also 19:9 salvation came today; 23:43 today you will be with me in paradise). The fulfillment is not a future event Jesus predicts but a present reality he declares happening as he speaks; the scripture's fulfillment is coterminous with his proclamation.
1: pleres pneumatos hagiou — full of the Holy Spirit — Luke adds pleres (full) to Mark's simpler account; the same adjective describes Stephen (Acts 6:5) and Barnabas (Acts 11:24), making Spirit-fullness a mark of God's commissioned agents. ēgeto en tō pneumati — was led by the Spirit — the passive ēgeto (imperfect, ongoing action) stresses that the wilderness journey is Spirit-driven, not a retreat. The eremos (wilderness/desert) echoes Israel's forty-year desert testing (Numbers 14:33; Deuteronomy 8:2).
2: hemeras tesserakonta — forty days — the number recapitulates Israel's forty-year wilderness test (Deuteronomy 8:2) and Moses's forty-day fast on Sinai (Exodus 34:28). peirazomenos hypo tou diabolou — being tempted by the devil — diabolos (slanderer, accuser) is Luke's preferred term (vs. satanas in Mark 1:13); it carries the forensic overtone of an adversarial prosecutor. The imperfect peirazomenos indicates ongoing pressure throughout the forty days, not just the three climactic exchanges that follow.
3: ei huios ei tou theou — if you are the Son of God — the conditional is first class in Greek, assuming the reality of the premise; the devil does not doubt the title but uses it to direct what the Son of God should do. eipe tō lithō toutō hina genētai artos — command this stone to become bread — the test targets trust in God's provision; Israel complained about lack of bread in the wilderness (Exodus 16:3; Numbers 11:4-6). The singular stone contrasts with Matthew's plural "these stones" (Matthew 4:3), focusing the personal challenge.
4: gegraptai hoti ouk ep artō monō zesetai ho anthrōpos — it is written: man shall not live on bread alone — Jesus cites Deuteronomy 8:3 from memory, the passage about YHWH's provision of manna and the purpose of the forty-year desert: to teach Israel to trust God's word over physical appetite. Luke's version omits Matthew's continuation ("but by every word that comes from the mouth of God"), keeping the focus on the sufficiency of the written word as a counter to the devil's premise. Gegraptai (it is written, perfect passive) frames scripture as settled, authoritative, and present-tense binding.
5: anagagōn auton edeixen autō pasas tas basileias tēs oikoumenēs — leading him up, he showed him all the kingdoms of the inhabited world — oikoumenē (the inhabited world, the Roman empire's self-description) implies political totality; Luke's version omits the specific "very high mountain" of Matthew. The display is instantaneous (en stigmē chronou = in a moment of time), suggesting visionary or supernatural perception rather than geographic vantage. Luke places this temptation second (Matthew places it third), centering the political-power temptation before the temple-leap.
6: soi dōsō tēn exousian tautēn hapasan kai tēn doxan autōn, hoti emoi paradedotai — I will give you all this authority and their glory, because it has been handed over to me — the devil claims delegated authority over the kingdoms: paradedotai (perfect passive, has been handed over) implies YHWH's permission, not self-seizure. This theological datum appears only in Luke; it frames earthly political power as operating within a sphere the devil administers — a sobering commentary on the nature of worldly authority. Exousia (authority) and doxa (glory) are the two components of what Gentile rulers pursued; the devil offers both.
7: sy oun ean proskunēsēs enōpion emou, estai sou pasa — if therefore you worship before me, it will all be yours — proskuneō (worship, do obeisance, prostrate) is the term used for proper worship of YHWH and of Jesus in Luke-Acts; the devil demands the same posture. The offer short-circuits the cross: all authority without suffering. The irony Luke's audience perceives: Jesus will receive all authority (Matthew 28:18; Acts 1:8) — but through death and resurrection, not demonic bargain.
8: gegraptai, Kyrion ton theon sou proskunēseis kai autō monō latreuseis — it is written: you will worship the Lord your God, and him only will you serve — Deuteronomy 6:13 (LXX), from the Shema context (Deuteronomy 6:4-15). Latreuseis (you will serve, do cultic service) is the term for Levitical temple service; exclusive liturgical devotion to YHWH is the counterpart to exclusive political authority offered by the devil. The citation repudiates the offer by grounding authority in worship rather than negotiation.
9: hēgagen de auton eis Ierousalem kai estēsen epi to pterygion tou hierou — he led him to Jerusalem and set him on the pinnacle of the temple — Luke's final temptation is in Jerusalem, not in Galilee (reversing Matthew's order), which serves Luke's theological geography: Jerusalem is the climax of every movement in his narrative (the Gospel ends with the disciples remaining in Jerusalem; Acts begins there). Pterygion tou hierou (pinnacle of the temple) likely refers to the southeast corner of Herod's temple complex where the Royal Stoa met the Kidron Valley far below.
10: tois angelois autou enteleistai peri sou tou diaphylaxai se — he will command his angels concerning you to guard you — Psalms 91:11-12 quoted by the devil as the basis for a trust-test: if Jesus is the Son of God, God's own promise guarantees protection from fall. The irony is that Ps 91 is a wisdom psalm about the one who trusts YHWH in genuine danger; the devil weaponizes it to encourage manufactured danger. The text of the citation is verbatim LXX except for the omission of "in all your ways" (Psalms 91:11b).
11: kai hoti epi cheirōn arousin se, mēpote proskopsēs pros lithon ton poda sou — and they will lift you up on their hands, so that you will not strike your foot against a stone — Psalms 91:12, completing the citation. The imagery is angelic protection: divine messengers sustaining the trusting one from stumbling. The devil's inversion: what the psalm celebrates as God's care for the endangered believer is here reframed as justification for self-induced catastrophe. Deliberate misreading of scripture is the third temptation's method.
12: kai apokritheis eipen autō ho Iēsous hoti eirētai, Ouk ekpeiraseis Kyrion ton theon sou — Jesus answered and said to him: it is said, you shall not put the Lord your God to the test — Deuteronomy 6:16 (LXX), citing Israel's testing of YHWH at Massah/Meribah (Exodus 17:1-7) where they demanded miraculous water as proof of God's presence. Ekpeiraseis (you will test, put to the test) is the compound form of peirazō, intensified by the prefix. Jesus refuses to weaponize God's faithfulness as a safety-net for manufactured crises.
13: kai syntelesas panta peirasmon ho diabolos apestē ap autou achri kairou — having completed every temptation, the devil departed from him until an opportune time — syntelesas panta peirasmon (having finished all the temptation) — Luke's phrase implies these three exchanges were representative of a comprehensive testing, not exhaustive in themselves. Achri kairou (until an opportune time, an appointed moment) is Lukan foreshadowing: in 22:3 Satan enters Judas and the Passion begins. The wilderness conflict is temporarily suspended, not resolved; the cross will be the decisive engagement.
14: kai hypestrepsen ho Iēsous en tē dynamei tou pneumatos eis tēn Galilaian — Jesus returned to Galilee in the power of the Spirit — dynamis (power) is Luke's key word for the Spirit's operative force (cf. Luke 1:35; Acts 1:8; Luke 10:38). The Spirit who led Jesus into the wilderness now empowers his Galilean ministry; the forty-day testing produced not defeat but intensified spiritual authority. phēmē exēlthen kath holēs tēs perichōrou — news went out through the whole surrounding countryside — phēmē (reputation, fame) spreads from first-hand witness before Jesus himself explains anything.
15: kai autos edidasken en tais synagōgais autōn doxazomenos hypo pantōn — and he was teaching in their synagogues, being glorified by all — the imperfect edidasken (was teaching) indicates repeated synagogue teaching across multiple towns; doxazomenos (being glorified, praised) reflects the audience's positive initial reception before the Nazareth controversy. The synagogue circuit (cf. Matthew 4:23; Mark 1:39) is Jesus's primary early platform: public reading and exposition of Torah within the covenant community.
16: kai ēlthen eis Nazara, hou ēn tethrammenos — he came to Nazareth, where he had been brought up — tethrammenos (perfect passive participle of trephō, to nurture, raise) emphasizes Nazareth as the site of Jesus's formation, not just his birthplace. Luke places this synagogue scene at the opening of the Galilean ministry (vs. Mark 6:1-6, which places it after many miracles); it functions as the programmatic statement of Jesus's mission before it plays out. kata to eiōthos autō — as was his custom — Jesus's synagogue attendance is habitual, not occasional; he is a Torah-observant Jew.
17: kai epedothē autō biblion tou prophētou Ēsaiou — the scroll of the prophet Isaiah was handed to him — the biblion (scroll, book) was the Isaiah scroll of the synagogue. The attendant (hyperetēs) would hand the scroll to the reader; the reader would stand to read and sit to expound. Luke notes that Jesus unrolled it (anaptuxas, unrolled/opened) and found (heuren) the place — a deliberate search, not random selection. The passage is Isaiah 61:1-2 with an insertion from Isaiah 58:6 ("to set the oppressed free").
19: kēruxai eniauton Kyriou dekton — to proclaim the year of the Lord's favor — the Jubilee year (Leviticus 25:8-55) is the economic-social institution in which debts are cancelled, slaves freed, and alienated land restored. Jesus does not announce a calendar Jubilee but the eschatological reality the Jubilee pointed toward: the age of God's acceptance and restorative mercy. Dekton (acceptable, favored, welcomed) is the quality of the era; Luke's theology of time presents this as semeron (today, Luke 4:21) — the present moment of proclamation is the era of favor.
20: ptuxas to biblion apodous tō hyperetē ekathisen — rolling up the scroll and giving it back to the attendant, he sat down — the seated posture was the authoritative teaching position for rabbis and synagogue teachers (cf. Matthew 5:1; Luke 23:2; Luke 5:3); standing was for public reading. The transition from standing reader to seated teacher signals that exposition is about to begin. kai pantōn hoi ophthalmoi en tē synagōgē ēsan atenizovtes autō — the eyes of all in the synagogue were fixed on him — atenizō (to fix one's eyes on, gaze intently) is a Lukan word for concentrated attention (Acts 1:10; Luke 3:4; Luke 6:15).
22: kai pantes emartyroyn autō kai ethaumazon epi tois logois tēs charitos — all bore witness to him and were amazed at the gracious words coming from his mouth — hoi logoi tēs charitos (the words of grace/gift) is unusual; it may mean words of gracious content (announcing favor) or words spoken with charis (rhetorical grace). The initial reception is positive — amazement — before the challenge. ouchi huios estin Iōsēph houtos — is this not Joseph's son? — the rhetorical question marks the audience's frame for Jesus: a hometown craftsman's son whose social standing is known. The question is incredulous, not hostile yet.
23: pantōs ereite moi tēn parabolēn tautēn, Iatre, therapeusen seauton — surely you will say to me this proverb: Physician, heal yourself — Jesus anticipates the Nazareth crowd's demand with a proverb known from Greek and Semitic sources (cf. b. Sanhedrin 17a). The implicit challenge: demonstrate at home what you have done elsewhere. Hosa ēkousamen genomena eis tēn Kapharnaoum, poiēson kai hōde — whatever we heard happened in Capernaum, do also here — the Nazareth audience has heard about Capernaum miracles (though Luke has not yet narrated them); the hometown crowd expects privileged access to divine power.
24: oudeis prophētēs dektos estin en tē patridi autou — no prophet is accepted in his hometown — dektos (acceptable, welcome) echoes Luke 4:19 ("the year of the Lord's favor/acceptance"); the Jubilee-favor pronounced on the poor is withheld from the prophet by his own town. This is not a proverb Jesus merely cites but a pattern he identifies as programmatic for his own ministry; prophets from Moses to Elijah faced rejection from Israel. Luke's amēn legō hymin (truly I tell you) marks the saying as solemn declaration.
25: pollai chērai ēsan en tais hēmerais Ēliou — there were many widows in Israel in the days of Elijah — Jesus cites 1 Kgs 17-18 to make the scandalous point that divine mercy bypassed the covenant people and went to a Gentile. The three-and-a-half-year drought (cf. James 5:17) created mass famine. Chērai (widows) are the paradigmatic vulnerable poor of the OT; their plight during the drought was Israel's judgment situation, yet Elijah was sent to none of them.
26: all epi Sarepta tēs Sidōnias pros gynaika chēran — but to Zarephath of Sidon, to a widow woman — Sarepta is in Phoenician territory, outside Israel's borders; the widow there is a Gentile. This is not a minor detail: YHWH deliberately bypassed the many needy Israelite widows and directed Elijah to a foreign woman. Luke uses this OT precedent to legitimize Jesus's ministry reaching beyond ethnic Israel — a theme that will climax in Acts. The pattern offends Nazareth precisely because it decentralizes covenant privilege.
27: kai polloi leproi ēsan epi Elisaiou tou prophētou en tō Israēl — and there were many lepers in Israel in the time of the prophet Elisha — 2 Kgs 5 is the parallel case: Naaman the Syrian (Aramean) general, not an Israelite, was the one cleansed. Oudeois autōn ekatharisthē ei mē Naiman ho Syros — none of them was cleansed except Naaman the Syrian. The point is double: divine healing power was available during Elisha's era, but it went to a Gentile military officer. The Nazareth crowd's fury (Luke 4:28) is the direct consequence of this implication for Jesus's ministry.
28: eplesthesan pantes thymon en tē synagōgē — all in the synagogue were filled with rage — thymos (wrath, rage, passionate anger) contrasts with the initial thaumazein (amazement) of Luke 4:22; the congregation moved from wonder to murderous fury in moments. The trigger was not an insult to their religion but the implication that God's eschatological favor might pass over Israel in favor of Gentiles — the scandal of divine election working beyond ethnic boundaries. Their fury is the first enactment of the rejection-of-the-prophet pattern Jesus just announced.
29: kai anastantes exebalon auton exō tēs poleōs kai ēgagon auton heōs ophryos tou orous — rising up, they drove him out of the city and led him to the brow of the hill on which the city was built — mob action: the congregation becomes a mob and drives Jesus from the synagogue, out of town, and to a cliff. Ophrys (brow, ridge, eyebrow) refers to the hill's edge above a precipice. Katakremnisai auton (to throw him down) was the method of summary execution by cliff-throwing used in Israel's antiquity (cf. 2 Chronicles 25:12); they intend to kill him.
30: autos de dielthōn dia mesou autōn eporeueto — but he passed through the middle of them and went on his way — the escape is narrated without explanation of method: no angelic intervention, no miraculous blindness is mentioned. The point is simply that Jesus departed; Luke's reticence is more impressive than Matthew's or John's (cf. John 8:59; Luke 10:39). His hour has not come (cf. John 7:30); the first attempt to kill him fails because the appointed time has not arrived. He "went on his way" (eporeueto imperfect = continued traveling) — the mission continues undeterred.
31: kai katēlthen eis Kapharnaoum polin tēs Galilaias — he went down to Capernaum, a city of Galilee — katēlthen (went down) reflects geography: Nazareth is at 370m elevation; Capernaum is on the north shore of the Sea of Galilee at -210m. Capernaum becomes Jesus's Galilean base (cf. Luke 4:23, where the Nazarenes anticipated this). Luke adds "a city of Galilee" — a Gentile reader's orientation note. kai ēn didaskōn autous en tois sabbasin — and he was teaching them on the Sabbaths — the imperfect indicates regular weekly synagogue teaching.
32: kai exeplēssonto epi tē didachē autou, hoti en exousia ēn ho logos autou — they were astonished at his teaching, because his word was with authority — exeplēssonto (were astonished, struck out of their senses) is a strong reaction verb. Exousia (authority) is the theological key: rabbis taught by citation of predecessors ("Rabbi X says..."); Jesus taught with direct pronouncement. His word carries its own authorization without appeal to tradition. Luke pairs exousia (authority) with dynamis (power) in Luke 4:36 — the conceptual twins of his ministry.
33: kai en tē synagōgē ēn anthrōpos echōn pneuma daimoniou akathartou — in the synagogue there was a man having a spirit of an unclean demon — the demon's presence in the synagogue is deliberate: the first confrontation occurs in the house of worship, not in the streets. Pneuma daimoniou akathartou is Luke's compound phrase combining daimonion (demon, divine being) and akathartos (unclean, ritually impure). The man's possession disrupts the covenant assembly. kai anekraxen phōnē megalē — and he cried out with a loud voice — the demon's involuntary confrontation of Jesus.
34: Ea, ti hēmin kai soi, Iēsou Nazarēne — Let us alone! What have you to do with us, Jesus of Nazareth? — Ea is an interjection of protest or alarm. Ti hēmin kai soi (what to us and to you? = what do we have to do with each other?) is a Semitic idiom of non-engagement (cf. Judges 11:12; 2 Samuel 16:10). The demon identifies Jesus geographically (Nazarēne) and with full theological precision: ho hagios tou theou — the Holy One of God — a unique Christological title combining holiness (consecrated, set apart) with divine origin. Demonic knowledge precedes human confession.
35: kai epitimēsen autō ho Iēsous legōn, Phimōthēti kai exelthe ap autou — Jesus rebuked him saying: Be muzzled and come out of him — epitimēsen (rebuked, charged sternly) is Luke's standard verb for Jesus's authoritative command over demons and natural forces (cf. 8:24 wind and waves; 4:39 fever). Phimōthēti (be silenced, be muzzled) suppresses both the demon's speech and its further activity. mēden blapsas auton — having harmed him in nothing — Luke's medical observation (cf. Mark 1:26 where convulsion and a loud cry are noted); Luke emphasizes the clean, harmless exit as evidence of complete divine control.
36: kai egeneto thambos epi pantas — astonishment came upon all — thambos (astonishment, wonder mixed with fear) is the reaction to the unprecedented. They interpret what they witnessed: hoti en exousia kai dynamei epitassei tois akathartois pneumasin — because with authority and power he commands the unclean spirits, and they come out. Exousia kai dynamis — authority and power — are Luke's paired theological concepts: exousia is the right to command; dynamis is the effective force executing the command. Both characterize Jesus's ministry throughout Luke-Acts.
37: kai exeporeueto ēchos peri autou eis panta topon tēs perichōrou — and a report about him went out to every place in the surrounding region — ēchos (echo, sound, report — Luke's medical vocabulary extended metaphorically) marks the spread of reputation without formal announcement. Perichōros (surrounding region) implies the countryside around Capernaum, including the Jordan Valley settlements. News of exorcisms by word alone was extraordinary; Jewish exorcism practices of the period involved elaborate rituals and incantations (cf. Josephus, Ant. 8.46-48).
38: anastas de apo tēs synagōgēs eisēlthen eis tēn oikian Simōnos — rising from the synagogue he entered Simon's house — the sequence is direct: synagogue → Simon's house in Capernaum. This is the first mention of Simon Peter's home in Luke (though Mark 1:29-31 introduces Simon earlier). penthera de tou Simōnos ēn synechomenē pyretō megalō — Simon's mother-in-law was being held by a great fever — synekhomenē (was being held, constrained, pressed) is a medical term; Luke adds megalō (great/high) to describe the fever's severity, consistent with Luke's medical vocabulary (pyretō megalō corresponds to what ancient physicians called a dangerous systemic fever).
39: kai epistas epanō autēs epetimēsen tō pyretō — standing over her, he rebuked the fever — the same verb epitimēsen used against the demon (Luke 4:35) is here applied to a fever — an unprecedented usage that treats disease as something subject to command and rebuke, not merely to medical treatment. Jesus does not administer medicine or pray; he commands the fever directly. kai aphēken autēn — and it left her — the fever's departure is described with the same verb (aphiēmi) used for the departure of demons; Luke treats the healing as a liberation. parachrēma de anastasa diēkonei autois — immediately she rose and was serving them — instant and complete restoration.
40: dynontos de tou hēliou hapantes hosoi eichon asthenountas nosois poikilais ēgagon autous pros auton — as the sun was setting, all who had sick people with various diseases brought them to him — the Sabbath ended at sunset; carrying the sick was prohibited on the Sabbath (m. Shabb. Luke 10:5), so the crowd waited until sunset before bringing the ill. The mass healing session is Lukan: ho de heni hekastō autōn tas cheiras epititheis etherapeuev autous — laying his hands on each one of them, he healed them — Luke's individual attention: not a collective healing wave but personal laying on of hands (cheiras epititheis) on each person, the apostolic gesture of conferring blessing and healing.
41: exērcheto de kai daimonia apo pollōn krazonta kai legonta hoti sy ei ho huios tou theou — demons were coming out of many, crying and saying: you are the Son of God — the collective demonic confession of Jesus's divine sonship (cf. Luke 4:34 "the Holy One of God") is the involuntary testimony of the spirit world to what the human world has not yet recognized. kai epitimōn ouk eia auta lalein, hoti ēdeisan ton Christon auton einai — rebuking them, he would not allow them to speak, because they knew him to be the Christ — the messianic secret: Jesus silences demonic identification of his identity. The reason Luke gives is theological: their knowledge of his Messianic role (not just his divine sonship) is what must not be premature announced.
42: genomenēs de hēmeras exelthōn eporeuthe eis erēmon topon — when day came, he went out and went to a solitary place — Luke's characteristic emphasis on Jesus at prayer: this verse in Luke becomes an explicit prayer scene in the parallel account's pattern (cf. Luke 5:16; Luke 6:12; Luke 9:18; Luke 11:1; Luke 22:41). The withdrawal to erēmos topos (deserted/solitary place) after a full day of healing and exorcism follows the pattern of Moses and Elijah in wilderness retreat. hoi de ochloi epezētoun auton — the crowds were seeking him — epizēteō (seek eagerly, search for) indicates pressing need; they tried to prevent him from departing.
43: ho de eipen pros autous hoti kai tais hetrais polesin euangelisasthai me dei tēn basileian tou theou — he said to them: I must proclaim the good news of the kingdom of God to the other towns also — dei (it is necessary, I must) is Luke's divine-compulsion word, marking what God has appointed and what Jesus cannot deviate from (cf. Luke 2:49; Luke 9:22; Luke 13:33; Luke 24:26,44). Euangelisasthai (to proclaim good news) is Luke's preferred verb for Jesus's mission; it occurs more in Luke-Acts than in all other NT books combined. hoti epi touto apestalmēn — because for this I was sent — the mission statement in brief: apostolic sending by the Father to proclaim the kingdom.
44: kai ēn kēryssōn eis tas synagōgas tēs Ioudaias — and he was preaching in the synagogues of Judea — Ioudaias here likely means the broader Jewish region (cf. 1:5 where "Judea" encompasses the whole land), not the southern province exclusively, since the preceding narrative is in Galilee. Luke uses kēryssōn (preaching, proclaiming as a herald) as a summary-verb for the Galilean circuit, distinguishing it from the more content-specific euangelizesthai (Luke 4:43). The imperfect tense continues the panoramic summary of the mission's scope.
